questions.question: A DFA has 4 states and processes a 10-symbol input string. How many state transitions occur?
options: 10 — one transition per input symbol, regardless of how many states exist
It depends on the number of accepting states
questions.explanation: A DFA reads one symbol per step and performs exactly one transition per symbol. A string of length n causes exactly n transitions, starting from q₀. The number of states in the DFA does not affect how many transitions occur — it affects which states are reachable.

questions.question: What does L(M) mean for a DFA M = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F)?
options: The set of all states in M
The set of all strings w ∈ Σ* such that δ*(q₀, w) ∈ F — i.e., strings that drive M to an accepting state
The set of all transitions in M
questions.explanation: L(M) is the language accepted by M: the set of all strings that, when processed symbol-by-symbol from the start state, leave the machine in one of the accepting states F. The extended transition function δ* applies δ repeatedly to consume the entire string.

questions.question: Given two DFAs M₁ (5 states) and M₂ (3 states) over the same alphabet, how many states does their product DFA (for intersection) have?
options: 15 — the product states are pairs (q₁, q₂) ∈ Q₁ × Q₂, so |Q₁| × |Q₂| = 5 × 3 = 15
It depends on the number of accepting states
questions.explanation: The product construction creates a new state for every pair (q₁, q₂) where q₁ ∈ Q₁ and q₂ ∈ Q₂. The total is |Q₁| × |Q₂|. For intersection, (q₁, q₂) is accepting iff q₁ ∈ F₁ AND q₂ ∈ F₂. For union, it is accepting iff q₁ ∈ F₁ OR q₂ ∈ F₂. The transition is δ((q₁,q₂), a) = (δ₁(q₁,a), δ₂(q₂,a)).

questions.question: How do you build the complement of a DFA M = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F)?
options: Keep Q, Σ, δ, and q₀ the same; set the new accepting states to Q \ F (swap accept and reject)
Add a new state that loops on all inputs
questions.explanation: Complementing a DFA is trivially easy because the machine is deterministic and total (every state has a transition for every symbol). You simply flip which states are accepting: the new F' = Q \ F. Every string that was accepted is now rejected and vice versa. Note: this does NOT work for NFAs without first converting to a DFA, because NFAs may have no transition for some (state, symbol) pairs.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a DFA and a general computer?
options: A DFA can process any input a computer can
A DFA has finite memory (no auxiliary storage beyond its current state)
A DFA is always faster than a general computer
A DFA can only process binary inputs
questions.explanation: A DFA has a fixed, finite number of states and no external memory. It reads input one symbol at a time and transitions based on current state and input. This limits it to recognizing regular languages — it cannot, for example, check if parentheses are balanced.
